title: SLAM Mapping Challenge
description: Implement and compare multiple SLAM algorithms on the same dataset or simulation environment. Map a complex indoor environment using at least two different SLAM approaches (e.g. LiDAR SLAM and visual SLAM), evaluate their performance quantitatively, and understand the trade-offs between different SLAM methods.
whyItMatters: SLAM is a cornerstone capability for autonomous mobile robots, and no single SLAM algorithm is best for all situations. Understanding the strengths and limitations of different approaches — LiDAR vs visual, feature-based vs direct, filter-based vs graph-based — enables you to select and configure the right SLAM system for a given application. This comparative understanding is what separates a competent robotics engineer from someone who just runs default configurations.
skillsLearned: Dataset recording and playback (ROS 2 bag files)
Point cloud and occupancy grid analysis
prerequisites: Understanding of SLAM concepts (occupancy grids, loop closure, graph optimisation)
Linux command line and Python scripting

steps.title: Create the test environment and dataset
steps.description: Build a Gazebo simulation world with varied features: long corridors, rooms of different sizes, loop-closure opportunities (paths that return to previously visited areas), and challenging areas (featureless walls, glass surfaces, repetitive patterns). Drive the robot through the environment while recording all sensor data to a ROS 2 bag file. Also record ground truth poses from the simulator.
steps.tips: Include at least one large loop in the trajectory to test loop closure. Add some dynamic elements (moving people or objects) to test robustness.

steps.title: Run LiDAR SLAM
steps.description: Configure and run SLAM Toolbox (or Cartographer) on the recorded LiDAR data. Tune parameters for your environment: scan matching resolution, loop closure thresholds, map update frequency. Save the resulting map and trajectory estimate.
steps.tips: SLAM Toolbox's 'localization' mode can be used after mapping to refine the trajectory estimate using the already-built map.

steps.title: Run Visual SLAM
steps.description: Configure and run a visual SLAM system (ORB-SLAM3 or RTAB-Map in visual mode) on the recorded camera data. If using ORB-SLAM3, you will need to configure camera parameters and vocabulary. Compare the visual SLAM trajectory and map with the LiDAR SLAM results.
steps.tips: RTAB-Map supports both visual and LiDAR SLAM and can fuse both, making it a good choice for this comparison project.

steps.title: Quantitative evaluation
steps.description: Compare both SLAM systems against ground truth using standard metrics: Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) — the global consistency of the estimated trajectory, and Relative Pose Error (RPE) — the local accuracy of motion estimation. Use the evo evaluation toolkit (pip install evo). Generate trajectory plots showing estimated vs ground truth trajectories for both methods.
steps.tips: Align the estimated trajectories to ground truth using Umeyama alignment before computing ATE. Report results as RMSE and median error.

steps.title: Analysis and reporting
steps.description: Write a comparative analysis: where does each method succeed and fail? How does each handle long corridors, sharp turns, loop closures, and featureless areas? What are the computational requirements (CPU usage, memory, processing time)? Which method would you recommend for a specific Australian application (e.g. underground mine mapping, warehouse navigation, outdoor agriculture)?
steps.tips: Include qualitative map comparison — overlay both maps and ground truth floor plan. Point out specific areas where one method outperforms the other.

deliverables: ROS 2 bag dataset with ground truth
Maps generated by at least two SLAM methods
Quantitative evaluation report with ATE and RPE metrics
Comparative analysis document with recommendations for different use cases
Trajectory visualisation plots and map overlays

rubric.description: Recorded dataset covers varied environments with loop closures, and ground truth is accurate
Both SLAM systems are properly configured and tuned, not just run with defaults
Evaluation uses correct metrics (ATE, RPE) with proper alignment, reported with statistical measures
Analysis identifies specific strengths and weaknesses of each method with evidence from the results
Clear recommendations for when to use each method, grounded in the evaluation results
